title: Creating Your First Unreal Project
learningTarget: I can create and save a new Unreal Engine project using the correct class setup, project name, and starter content settings.

bellRinger.prompt: What kinds of digital worlds have you seen in games, movies, architecture, or VR?

vocabulary.definition: A real-time 3D creation tool used for games, film, visualization, and interactive media.
The launcher used to install and open Unreal Engine.
The startup window where users open recent projects or create new ones.
A project template that starts with minimal prebuilt content.
Sample assets that can be added to a new project for practice.
Unreal visual scripting used instead of writing C++ for many beginner interactions.

slides.title: Lesson 01 - Creating Your First Unreal Project

assignment.title: First Unreal Project Setup
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of project browser or open Unreal project
Screenshot or written confirmation showing correct project name

exitTicket: What setting should be enabled if we want starter assets available in the project?

title: Unreal Interface Tour
learningTarget: I can identify the main Unreal interface panels and explain how each one supports building and organizing a 3D world.

bellRinger.prompt: Which software interface have you used that felt complicated at first, and what helped you learn it?

vocabulary.definition: The main window that shows the 3D world and lets users select or manipulate objects.
The row of tools used to add objects, switch modes, play, and access common actions.
A list of objects in the current level, useful for selecting, organizing, hiding, and grouping.
The panel that shows editable properties for the selected object.
The asset browser that can open from the bottom of the editor, often with Ctrl+Space.
The docked version of the Content Drawer that shows project folders and assets.

assignment.title: Unreal Interface Map
evidenceRequired: Annotated screenshot or form response identifying viewport, toolbar, outliner, details panel, and content drawer

exitTicket: Which panel shows the selected object's editable settings?

title: Viewport Navigation
learningTarget: I can navigate the Unreal viewport confidently using mouse controls, keyboard movement, focus, view modes, show flags, and game view.

bellRinger.prompt: Why might navigation controls matter before building anything in a 3D scene?

vocabulary.definition: The controls used to move the editor camera around the 3D world.
A first-person style movement method used while holding the right mouse button.
The setting that controls how fast the viewport camera moves.
The F key action that snaps the viewport to the selected object.
A viewport display mode such as Lit, Unlit, or Wireframe.
A viewport option toggled with G that hides editor-only widgets and shows what the player would see.

assignment.title: Viewport Navigation Scavenger Hunt
evidenceRequired: Screenshots from at least three different views or camera positions
One screenshot showing game view or a different view mode

exitTicket: What does pressing G toggle in the viewport?

title: Moving, Rotating, and Scaling Objects
learningTarget: I can build a simple starter scene by moving, rotating, scaling, duplicating, and placing objects from the toolbar and Content Drawer.

bellRinger.prompt: What is the difference between moving an object and changing the camera view?

vocabulary.definition: Move an object along one or more axes.
Turn an object around an axis.
Change the size or proportions of an object.
The colored handles used to move, rotate, or scale selected objects.
A setting that moves, rotates, or scales objects in set increments.
Moving or rotating based on the selected object direction instead of the world direction.

slides.title: Lesson 04 - Moving, Rotating, and Scaling Objects

assignment.title: Starter Scene Transform Challenge
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of completed simple scene
Short reflection naming at least three tools or shortcuts used

exitTicket: What are the W, E, and R shortcuts used for in Unreal?

title: Quiz 1 and First Scene Cleanup

video.note: Review range only; no new required tutorial segment.

learningTarget: I can show what I remember from Lessons 01-04 and improve my first Unreal scene using feedback, organization, and stronger evidence screenshots.

bellRinger.prompt: Which Unreal tool or shortcut from Lessons 01-04 helped you the most so far, and why?

vocabulary.definition: A focused check of earlier skills and vocabulary so you know what is solid and what still needs practice.
Information from a teacher, peer, rubric, or self-check that helps you decide what to improve.
A purposeful change that makes the work clearer, stronger, or more complete.
A screenshot, file, or written explanation that proves a skill was used.
A polished image of work that could be used later to show growth or achievement.

slides.title: Lesson 05 - Quiz 1 and First Scene Cleanup

assignment.title: First Scene Cleanup and Evidence Upgrade
evidenceRequired: Before screenshot, if available.
After screenshot.
Short reflection naming at least three tools or shortcuts.
Optional quiz completion screenshot or teacher checkoff.

exitTicket: What is one improvement you made to your first Unreal scene today?

title: Exposure and Post-Process

video.note: Covers auto exposure, a hut/dark-space test, manual exposure, post-process volume settings, bloom, vignette, saturation, and Infinite Extent Unbound.

learningTarget: I can use a post-process volume to control exposure and camera-based visual effects in an Unreal scene.

bellRinger.prompt: Why does a camera or your eyes sometimes need time to adjust when moving from a bright place to a dark place?

vocabulary.definition: How bright or dark the rendered scene appears to the camera.
A camera behavior that automatically adjusts brightness, similar to eyes adapting between dark and bright areas.
An Unreal volume that applies camera and image effects after the scene is rendered.
A glow effect around bright areas of the image.
A darkening or shading effect around the edges of the image.
The strength or intensity of colors in the image.
A Post Process Volume setting that makes the effect apply to the entire level instead of only inside the volume.
The exposure measurement mode used to decide how scene brightness should be handled.

assignment.title: Post-Process Exposure Test
evidenceRequired: Screenshot showing auto exposure/problem situation.
Screenshot showing manual exposure/post-process result.
Short reflection explaining what changed.

exitTicket: What setting allows a post-process volume to affect the whole level?

title: Materials Basics

video.note: Covers material example setup, starter materials, creating a material, the material graph, Base Color, Roughness, Metallic, PBR, Apply/Save, and simple texture-driven variation.

learningTarget: I can create a basic Unreal material and control how it looks using base color, roughness, metallic, and simple texture-driven variation.

bellRinger.prompt: How can you tell whether a surface is metal, plastic, stone, wood, or paint just by looking at it?

vocabulary.definition: An Unreal asset that defines how a surface looks when rendered.
The node-based workspace used to build and edit a material.
A block in the graph that stores or processes information before sending it to a material input.
The main visible color of a material before lighting effects are applied.
A value that controls how sharp or blurry reflections appear on a surface.
A value that controls whether a surface behaves like metal or a non-metal.
Physically based rendering, a workflow that uses realistic material values such as metallic and roughness.
A single number value, often used for settings such as Roughness or Metallic.
A multi-number value often used to represent RGB color.
The material editor action that updates the material so changes appear in the level.

assignment.title: First Custom Material
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the material graph.
Screenshot of the material applied to an object.
Short reflection explaining base color, roughness, and metallic.

exitTicket: What happens if you change a material but forget to press Apply?

title: Texture Maps and Normal Maps

video.note: Covers importing texture maps, Base Color maps, Roughness maps, Normal maps, sRGB settings, mask/non-color texture settings, normal map compression, material connections, Texture Coordinate, Multiply, tiling/scale, and optional color tint. Ends before material instances.

learningTarget: I can import texture maps and connect base color, roughness, and normal maps correctly to build a more believable material.

bellRinger.prompt: What is the difference between color detail and surface depth detail?

vocabulary.definition: An image file used by a material to control part of the surface appearance.
A color texture that provides the main visible color detail of a material.
A grayscale texture that controls which parts of a surface look shiny or rough.
A texture that fakes small bumps and surface direction changes so lighting reacts with more detail.
A color interpretation setting that should be on for color textures and off for non-color data maps.
A non-color texture that stores control information, often in black, white, or grayscale values.
An import setting that tells Unreal how to treat and optimize a texture file.
A node that controls how texture UVs are read by a material.
The 2D coordinate layout used to place a texture on a 3D surface.
How often a texture repeats across a surface.

slides.title: Lesson 08 - Texture Maps and Normal Maps

assignment.title: Texture Map Material Build
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of imported texture assets.
Screenshot of material graph connections.
Screenshot of material applied in the level.
Short reflection explaining what the normal map adds.

exitTicket: Why should sRGB usually be turned off for roughness maps and normal maps?

title: Quiz 2 and Material Instances

video.note: Covers creating material instances from parent materials, exposing scalar/vector parameters, Apply/Save behavior, real-time material instance adjustments, normal flatness, roughness strength, color tint, resetting defaults, and efficient material variation. Includes Quiz 2 as the opening checkpoint.

learningTarget: I can explain core material concepts and create an editable material instance.

bellRinger.prompt: Why might a studio want many variations of one material?

vocabulary.definition: A child version of a material that can expose editable settings without rebuilding the whole material graph.
The original material that controls the structure and available parameters for its instances.
A material value exposed so it can be edited from a material instance.
A single-number parameter, often used for values such as size, roughness strength, or normal flatness.
A multi-value parameter often used for color tint or RGB values.
A texture slot exposed so a material instance can swap a texture map.
An enabled material instance value that replaces the parent material default.
A change that appears immediately in the viewport without recompiling the full material.
Returning an overridden material instance value back to the parent material value.
The material editor action that updates the parent material so new parameters appear in instances.

assignment.title: Material Instance Variation Set
evidenceRequired: Quiz 2 completion record or teacher checkoff.
Screenshot of the parent material showing at least two exposed parameters.
Screenshot of at least two material instance variations applied in the level.
Short reflection explaining how a material instance is different from a separate new material.

exitTicket: How is a material instance different from a separate new material?

title: Master Materials

video.note: Covers the master material concept, neutral default values, exposing texture parameters, swapping Base Color/Roughness/Normal textures in instances, adding a Metallic map, using a Static Switch Parameter for metallic behavior, and building a reusable material workflow.

learningTarget: I can describe why master materials help organize reusable material workflows.

bellRinger.prompt: What kinds of settings would be useful to reuse across many objects?

vocabulary.definition: A reusable parent material designed to drive many material instances.
A child material variation that uses exposed parameters from a parent or master material.
The value a parameter uses before an instance overrides it.
A texture input exposed so instances can swap maps without editing the parent graph.
A parameter that turns a material branch on or off, such as using a metallic map only when needed.
A grayscale or mask texture that controls which parts of a material behave like metal.
A non-color texture that stores control data for a material.
One texture that stores multiple masks in separate red, green, and blue channels.
A setup that saves time because the same structure can support many assets.
A consistent naming pattern such as M_ for materials and MI_ for material instances.

assignment.title: Starter Master Material Variations
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the master material or teacher-provided master material parameters.
Screenshot of a material instance with texture or value parameters enabled.
Screenshot of at least two applied material variations in the level.
Short reflection explaining one benefit of a master material.

exitTicket: What is one benefit of using a master material?

title: Importing Static Meshes

video.note: Covers static meshes as 3D objects, FBX import workflow, folder organization, import options, importing textures manually, checking sRGB/compression settings, creating a material instance from the master material, assigning a default material slot in the Static Mesh Editor, and introducing packed mask channel use.

learningTarget: I can import a static mesh and place it correctly in an Unreal scene.

bellRinger.prompt: What makes an asset ready or not ready to use in a project?

vocabulary.definition: A 3D object asset made of vertices and faces that can be placed in an Unreal level.
A common 3D model file format used to move static meshes between tools and Unreal.
Settings Unreal shows when bringing a file such as an FBX into the project.
The panel where imported assets, folders, materials, textures, and meshes are organized.
The editor used to inspect a static mesh and set options such as material slots.
A place on a mesh where a material can be assigned.
The material assigned to the mesh asset so new placed copies use that material automatically.
A group of related texture maps used together on one material or asset.
A texture that stores multiple masks in separate RGB channels.
A quick review to see whether an imported mesh is sized correctly in the scene.

assignment.title: Static Mesh Import and Material Setup
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the imported static mesh in the Content Drawer.
Screenshot of the mesh placed in the scene.
Screenshot of the material instance or default material slot used on the mesh.
Short reflection explaining what to check when an imported mesh looks wrong.

exitTicket: What should you check if an imported mesh looks too large or too small?

title: Migrating Assets Between Projects

video.note: Covers opening a source project, identifying a folder of assets, using the Migrate command, reviewing the asset dependency list, choosing the destination project Content folder, confirming migration success, locating the migrated folder in the destination project, and using Show in Explorer as an alternate way to find the Content folder. Ends before the Lumen lighting explanation.

learningTarget: I can migrate assets between Unreal projects while preserving dependencies.

bellRinger.prompt: Why might copying only one file fail when moving assets between projects?

vocabulary.definition: The Unreal workflow for moving assets from one project to another while including needed dependencies.
A related asset that another asset needs in order to work correctly, such as a material or texture.
The project that currently contains the assets being moved.
The project receiving the migrated assets.
The Unreal project folder where migrated assets must be placed.
The migration window that lists assets and dependencies Unreal will copy.
A command that opens the selected project folder location in Windows File Explorer.
The .uproject file used to open an Unreal project and locate its folders.
Checking that migrated assets appear and function correctly in the destination project.
A missing connection that can happen when an asset is copied without its dependencies.

assignment.title: Asset Migration Verification
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the source project asset or folder selected for migration.
Screenshot of the migration asset list or confirmation step.
Screenshot of the migrated folder or assets in the destination project.
Screenshot of at least one migrated object placed or opened successfully.
Short reflection explaining why dependencies matter.

exitTicket: Why does Unreal migrate dependencies with an asset?

title: Quiz 3 and Lumen Lighting

video.note: Covers Lumen as Unreal Engine 5 dynamic global illumination, light bounce, color bleed, static baked light maps, broken static lighting when objects move, movable lights, Lumen on/off comparison, source radius, softer shadows, and emissive material lighting. Includes Quiz 3 as the opening checkpoint.

learningTarget: I can explain the basic purpose of Lumen and use lighting evidence to show how global illumination changes an Unreal scene.

bellRinger.prompt: What makes lighting in a 3D scene feel realistic instead of flat?

vocabulary.definition: Unreal Engine 5 system for dynamic global illumination and reflections.
Light that bounces around a scene instead of stopping after one hit.
Light or scene information that appears on a shiny surface.
Light that reaches an area after bouncing from another surface.
Lighting that updates while objects or lights move.
Camera brightness control that changes how light or dark a scene appears.
A darker area created when light is blocked.
Screenshots or notes that prove how lighting changed the scene.
A baked texture that stores static lighting information.
A light that can update dynamically instead of relying on baked lighting.

assignment.title: Lumen Lighting Comparison
evidenceRequired: Quiz 3 completion record or teacher checkoff.
Google Drive link to a screenshot showing a lighting setup before adjustment.
Google Drive link to a screenshot showing improved Lumen or global illumination result.
Short reflection explaining how lighting changed the scene.

exitTicket: What is one thing Lumen helps simulate, and how can you prove it with evidence?

title: Light Types and Scene Lighting

video.note: Covers point lights, intensity, source radius, temperature, light color, indirect lighting intensity, spotlights, inner and outer cone angles, rectangle lights, directional lights as sunlight, source angle, skylights, sky atmosphere, and Lumen settings in a post-process volume.

learningTarget: I can use different Unreal light types to create a clear mood and visual focus in a scene.

bellRinger.prompt: Where do you see different types of light in real life, and how do they change the mood of a space?

vocabulary.definition: A light that emits from one point in all directions, like a bulb.
A light that shines in one direction inside a cone shape.
A light that emits from a rectangular surface in one direction.
A light used for sun-like light from a far distance.
A light that captures the sky or distant scene and applies it to the world.
The strength or power of a light.
A warm or cool color quality of light.
A darker area created when light is blocked.
The feeling created by lighting, color, contrast, and focus.
The part of the scene the viewer should notice first.

slides.title: Lesson 14 - Light Types and Scene Lighting

assignment.title: Three-Light Mood Study
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot of setup using point light.
Google Drive link to a screenshot of setup using spotlight or rectangle light.
Google Drive link to a screenshot of setup using directional light, skylight, or environment lighting.
Short reflection explaining which setup created the strongest mood and why.

exitTicket: Which light type helped your scene most today, and what did it change visually?

title: Landscapes

video.note: Covers creating a new map for landscapes, entering Landscape mode, creating a landscape, sculpting terrain, raising and lowering with mouse/Shift, brush size, falloff, smooth brush, and flatten brush for terraces or playable areas.

learningTarget: I can create, sculpt, and shape a basic Unreal landscape with intentional terrain forms.

bellRinger.prompt: What makes outdoor terrain look natural instead of flat or artificial?

vocabulary.definition: A terrain surface used to build outdoor ground in Unreal.
The shape and surface of outdoor ground such as hills, valleys, and cliffs.
A landscape tool action that raises or lowers terrain.
A tool that softens sharp or bumpy terrain changes.
A tool that creates a more level area from a chosen height.
A natural wearing-down effect or terrain idea used to make landforms feel less artificial.
The height of terrain compared with nearby areas.
How wide the landscape tool affects the terrain.
How strongly the tool changes terrain.
How large the terrain feels compared with the player or objects.

assignment.title: Sculpted Terrain Study
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot of Landscape mode or landscape setup.
Google Drive link to a wide shot of sculpted terrain.
Google Drive link to a close-up showing terrain shaping or refinement.
Short reflection naming the landscape tools used.

exitTicket: Which landscape tool changed your terrain most clearly, and how?

title: Foliage, Mini-World, and Blueprint Preview

video.note: Primary range covers adding trees to Foliage mode, density adjustment, Align to Normal, mobility, scale checks with the mannequin, paint and single instance placement, moving selected foliage, LOD/performance awareness, and play testing the world. Blueprint preview connection references 02:56:37-03:20:24, and final playable-space detailing references 04:57:25-04:59:31.

learningTarget: I can add environmental detail to a small Unreal scene and explain how foliage, props, and simple interaction prepare a scene for a playable mini-world.

bellRinger.prompt: What details make an environment feel alive or intentionally designed?

vocabulary.definition: Trees, grass, plants, and similar environment detail placed across a scene.
A placed object that adds detail, function, or story to a scene.
A focused improvement pass that adds believable detail to an environment.
The route a player is encouraged to follow through a space.
The area the viewer or player should notice first.
How many foliage instances appear in an area.
Differences in scale, rotation, color, or placement that make repeated assets feel natural.
A visual scripting asset used to create behavior or interaction in Unreal.
A volume or event that starts an action when something overlaps or activates it.
An area designed to be explored or used by a player.

slides.title: Lesson 16 - Foliage, Mini-World, and Blueprint Preview

assignment.title: Mini-World Environment Pass
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a wide screenshot of mini-world environment.
Google Drive link to a close-up screenshot showing foliage or prop detail.
Google Drive link to a screenshot or written description of simple interaction or Blueprint preview.
Short reflection explaining how the scene became more believable or playable.

exitTicket: What one detail made your mini-world feel more believable or playable?

title: Unreal Reboot and Production Expectations

video.note: Students build a castle environment in Unreal Engine while recording daily screen/camera evidence for a making-of documentary.

learningTarget: I can restart my Unreal workflow, set up my Q3 castle project, and begin documenting the production process with camera or screen-recorded evidence.

bellRinger.prompt: What should a behind-the-scenes documentary show that a final screenshot cannot show?

vocabulary.definition: A dated record of what was built, what was captured, what changed, and what needs to happen next.
Video captured from the computer screen to show workflow, testing, or before/after changes.
Camera or screen-recorded evidence that shows how a project was made.
Supporting footage that helps explain a process, place, detail, or action.
The Q3 Unreal scene students build, refine, walk through, and document.

slides.title: Q3 Lesson 01 - Unreal Reboot and Production Expectations

assignment.title: Q3 Production Project Setup
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the Q3 project setup.
Written production goal for the castle environment.
Daily documentary clip or screen recording showing the setup process.
Production log entry for today.

exitTicket: What did you capture today that would help someone understand the beginning of your castle project?

Video captured from the computer screen to show workflow, testing, or before/after changes.
Camera or screen-recorded evidence that shows how a project was made.
Supporting footage that helps explain a process, place, detail, or action.

title: Trees and Foliage

title: Video Production File Organization

video.source: Teacher-created Video Production File Organization Assignment
video.note: Students organize a messy video production file set into a professional folder structure before beginning DaVinci Resolve work.

learningTarget: I can organize video production files into a professional project folder structure so footage, audio, graphics, documents, assets, and exports are easy to find.

bellRinger.prompt: What is one problem that can happen when a video editor keeps footage, audio, graphics, and exports all in one messy folder?

vocabulary.definition: The main folder that contains every file needed for a production.
Recorded video clips captured by a camera, phone, drone, or other source.
The editable file created by software such as DaVinci Resolve, Premiere Pro, Final Cut Pro, or After Effects.
A rendered video file made from the editing project, often shared as a draft or final version.
A reusable production file such as a stock clip, template, texture, logo, or graphic.
A consistent naming system that makes files easier to identify, sort, and recover.

assignment.title: Video Production Folder Organization
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot showing the completed video production folder tree.
Google Drive or Docs link listing where at least twelve sample files were placed and why.
Short reflection explaining two organization decisions that would help an editor work faster.

exitTicket: Which file category was easiest to identify today, and which category required the most careful thinking?

title: Video Game Development File Organization

video.source: Teacher-created Video Game Development File Organization Assignment
video.note: Students organize a messy game development file set into a professional game project folder structure.

learningTarget: I can organize game development files into a professional project folder structure so art, audio, code, levels, documents, exports, plugins, and prefabs are easy to find.

bellRinger.prompt: What could go wrong in a game project if code, levels, art, audio, plugins, and builds are all mixed together?

vocabulary.definition: The main folder that contains the files needed to build and manage a game project.
A visual file used in a game, such as a model, texture, UI image, or environment object.
A reusable game object or asset setup that can be placed into a level.
A file that stores a game map, scene, or playable area.
An exported playable version of a game, such as a Windows executable or Android package.
An add-on that extends the game engine or project with extra features.

slides.title: Lesson 02 - Video Game Development File Organization

assignment.title: Video Game Development Folder Organization
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot showing the completed game development folder tree.
Google Drive or Docs link listing where at least twelve sample files were placed and why.
Short reflection explaining two organization decisions that would help a game team work faster.

exitTicket: Which file type gave you the clearest clue about where it belonged, and which file was most ambiguous?

title: DaVinci Resolve Setup and Project Manager

video.note: Introduces DaVinci Resolve as an all-in-one post-production tool, system expectations, free versus Studio awareness, Resolve pages, Project Manager, new project creation, project backup/export habits, and first workspace orientation.

learningTarget: I can create a DaVinci Resolve project, identify the main Resolve pages, and explain why project organization matters before editing begins.

bellRinger.prompt: What is one problem that can happen if an editor starts cutting video before naming, saving, and organizing the project?

vocabulary.definition: A video post-production application used for editing, visual effects, graphics, and delivery.
The startup area used to create, open, back up, and organize Resolve projects.
The database or location where Resolve stores project files and timelines.
A dedicated workspace inside Resolve, such as Media, Edit, Fusion, or Deliver.
The work that happens after footage is recorded, including editing, sound, graphics, and export.
The visible panels and tools arranged for a specific editing task.

slides.title: Lesson 01 - DaVinci Resolve Setup and Project Manager

assignment.title: Resolve Project Setup Proof
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot of the named Resolve project or Project Manager entry.
Google Drive or Docs link showing four Resolve pages with short purpose notes.
Short reflection explaining why project setup matters before editing.

exitTicket: Which Resolve page or startup area did you use today, and what job does it help an editor do?

title: Media Page, Imports, and Bins

video.note: Covers the Media page, media storage, Media Pool, importing clips, changing frame-rate prompts, bins, folder structure, list and thumbnail views, metadata, keywords, and smart-bin awareness.

learningTarget: I can import footage into the Media Pool and organize it with bins so my edit can be built from clean source material.

bellRinger.prompt: When you have a folder full of random clips, what information helps you find the right shot quickly?

vocabulary.definition: The Resolve workspace focused on finding, previewing, importing, and organizing media.
The panel used to browse files on the computer or connected drives.
The project area that contains clips, audio, timelines, and bins available to the edit.
A folder inside the Resolve project used to organize media.
Information about a clip, such as shot, scene, take, comments, or technical details.
A bin that gathers clips automatically based on rules or metadata filters.

slides.title: Lesson 02 - Media Page, Imports, and Bins

assignment.title: Organized Media Pool
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot of the Media Pool with organized bins.
Google Drive or Docs link showing at least two metadata or note entries.
Short reflection explaining one organization choice that will speed up editing.

exitTicket: What is the difference between media sitting on the computer and media imported into the Resolve Media Pool?

title: Syncing Audio and Timeline Basics

video.note: Introduces audio sync using waveform matching, switching to the Edit page, source viewer versus timeline viewer, adding clips to a timeline, playhead movement, track basics, and waveform awareness.

learningTarget: I can create a simple Resolve timeline, place clips in order, and explain the difference between source clips and the timeline edit.

bellRinger.prompt: Why might camera audio and separate microphone audio need to be matched before editing?

vocabulary.definition: The process of matching separate audio and video so dialogue or sound lines up correctly.
A visual shape that shows the loud and quiet parts of audio.
The Resolve page used to build and refine a story in a timeline.
The viewer used to preview a clip before adding it to the timeline.
The viewer that shows the edited sequence at the playhead.
The marker that shows the current time position in a clip or timeline.
A horizontal lane in the timeline that holds video or audio clips.

slides.title: Lesson 03 - Syncing Audio and Timeline Basics

assignment.title: First Timeline Assembly
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot of a timeline with at least four clips.
Google Drive or Docs link labeling source viewer, timeline viewer, playhead, and tracks.
Short reflection explaining one timeline decision.

exitTicket: How do the source viewer and timeline viewer help with different editing decisions?

title: Trimming Clips and Building a Rough Cut

video.note: Covers trimming clip edges, removing excess slate or dead time, choosing story moments, setting in and out points, adding selected ranges to a timeline, insert and append edits, trim mode, ripple trimming, split edits, and keyboard shortcut awareness.

learningTarget: I can trim clips, set in and out points, and build a rough cut that communicates a simple story without unnecessary dead space.

bellRinger.prompt: What makes a video clip feel too long, even if the shot itself is useful?

vocabulary.definition: To shorten or extend a clip by adjusting where it starts or ends.
The selected start of the part of a source clip that will be used.
The selected end of the part of a source clip that will be used.
An early version of an edit focused on order, timing, and story instead of polish.
An edit that places a clip and pushes later material down the timeline.
A trim that closes or opens timeline space so clips stay connected.
A cut that divides one clip into separate pieces.

slides.title: Lesson 04 - Trimming Clips and Building a Rough Cut

assignment.title: Rough Cut Story Sequence
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot of the rough cut timeline.
Google Drive or YouTube link to a short exported rough cut if teacher requests export.
Short reflection naming at least three tools or shortcuts used.

exitTicket: Name one trimming tool or shortcut you used today and explain what it changed in your rough cut.

title: Quiz 1 and Rough Cut Cleanup

video.note: Uses the first quiz as an opening checkpoint, then reinforces rough-cut cleanup with timeline navigation, audio level awareness, clip selection, unlinking audio only when needed, fades, the inspector, and a more intentional rough cut.

learningTarget: I can show what I know from the first Resolve lessons and clean up a rough cut so it is easier to understand.

bellRinger.prompt: What is one issue in your rough cut that a viewer would notice immediately?

vocabulary.definition: A short assessment used to check core vocabulary and workflow understanding.
The loudness of a clip or track.
A gradual change into or out of video or audio.
The panel used to adjust properties of a selected clip, title, effect, or audio item.
Video and audio that stay selected or moved together unless unlinked.
A focused editing pass that removes obvious problems before adding polish.

slides.title: Lesson 05 - Quiz 1 and Rough Cut Cleanup

assignment.title: Rough Cut Cleanup and Quiz 1 Checkpoint
evidenceRequired: Quiz 1 completion record or teacher checkoff.
Google Drive link to a screenshot of the cleaned rough cut timeline.
Google Drive or YouTube link to the rough cut if teacher requests export.
Short reflection naming at least three tools or shortcuts used.

exitTicket: What did you clean up today, and how does the rough cut communicate more clearly now?

title: Titles, Transitions, and Simple Motion

video.note: Covers the Effects panel, transitions, generators, titles, Fusion titles awareness, basic text title controls, inspector adjustments, position settings, simple keyframes, and using titles or motion only where they support the edit.

learningTarget: I can add a readable title or lower third and use a transition or simple motion choice only when it supports the video.

bellRinger.prompt: When does a title help a video, and when can it distract from the video?

vocabulary.definition: The panel that contains transitions, titles, generators, and other edit-page effects.
A visual change between two clips, such as a dissolve or wipe.
Generated text placed over or between video clips.
A title placed in the lower part of the screen, often used for names or labels.
A generated visual element such as a color or gradient.
A point in time that stores a value so an effect or property can change over time.
The panel used to adjust selected titles, clips, transitions, and effects.

slides.title: Lesson 06 - Titles, Transitions, and Simple Motion

assignment.title: Title and Transition Polish Pass
evidenceRequired: Google Drive link to a screenshot showing the title or lower third selected in the timeline.
Google Drive or YouTube link to a short clip preview if teacher requests export.
Short reflection explaining why the title or transition supports the story.

exitTicket: What title, transition, or motion choice did you add, and why does it belong in the edit?

title: Quiz 2 and Final Export

video.note: Uses Quiz 2 as the closing checkpoint, then covers the Deliver page, render settings, single clip export, codec awareness, H.264/H.265/ProRes comparison, file naming, render queue, multiple versions, and final reflection.

learningTarget: I can complete a final checkpoint and export a small edited video with clear naming, useful render settings, and a reflection on my post-production workflow.

bellRinger.prompt: What needs to be true before a project is ready to export for someone else to watch?

vocabulary.definition: The Resolve page used to render or export a timeline into a video file.
To convert the timeline into a finished video file.
A method for compressing and storing video or audio data.
A common delivery codec that is widely compatible for online video.
A newer efficient codec that can make smaller high-quality files but may have compatibility limits.
A high-quality editing and mastering codec that usually creates larger files.
The list of export jobs waiting to render in Resolve.
Rendering the whole timeline as one video file.

assignment.title: Final Resolve Mini-Edit Export
evidenceRequired: Quiz 2 completion record or teacher checkoff.
Google Drive or YouTube link to the final exported mini-edit.
Google Drive link to a screenshot of the Deliver page or render queue.
Short reflection explaining the most important editing, audio, title, or export decision.

exitTicket: What export setting or file naming choice do you need to remember for future video projects?
